text: The laparoscopic view shows the sigmoid colon protruding into the indirect inguinal hernia. The surgeon applies external manual pressure to reduce the hernia, partially reducing the sigmoid colon, but a tennis ball-sized portion remains incarcerated. The surgeon attempts robotic maneuvers to safely reduce the hernia without converting to an open procedure.

The robotic system is docked, with the surgeon using a fenestrated bipolar in the left hand and scissors in the right hand. The surgical field shows no left inguinal hernia. A small red dot is visible from the injection of marking in the preperitoneal space near the ASIS. The surgeon begins creating a peritoneal flap, avoiding the retro-rectal space due to visible inferior epigastric vessels in the thin male patient. The dissection proceeds in the Bogros space, staying close to the peritoneum to facilitate separation of the testicular vessels, while being cautious of the sigmoid colon and retroperitoneal fat due to the slider hernia.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis, revealing the differentiation between the testicular vessels and colon tissue. The transverse salus sling, a robust structure anchoring the mesh, is visible. The surgeon carefully detaches the sling's attachments from the sac, attempting to mobilize the sigmoid colon, which remains partially stuck. The bedside assistant retracts, allowing a wad of tissue to pass through, while a portion of the colon remains adhered. The surgeon avoids excessive force to prevent damage, focusing on detaching retroperitoneal attachments to the testicular vessels.

The surgeon dissects the space of Retsius, attempting to visualize the superior pubic rami. Despite the patient's thin physique, there is a notable amount of fat anterior to the retro-rectus area. The surgeon identifies tissue attachments from the transverse salus sling and transverse abdominus muscle that are holding the hernia sac in place, which need to be carefully detached.

The surgeon uses cautery to carefully dissect and remove anterior medial attachments from the hernia sac, ensuring the colon is not thermally injured. The transverse salus fascia is visible during this process. The testicular vessels are identified and separated to prevent bleeding and facilitate the reduction of the hernia, making the sac and colon more mobile.

The surgeon creates a large pocket for mesh placement, ensuring good posterior lateral overlap. The testicular vessels are visible, having been separated from the sigmoid colon to prevent them from being part of the mass being reduced. Additional attachments are divided, allowing structures to be freed without external pressure. The surgeon identifies the plane between the hernia sac and testicular vessels, facilitating dissection along this plane. Scar tissue from the hernia sac is visible, and the sac is safely dissected off the testicular vessels and vas deferens, with careful attention to avoid injuring any vessels.

The surgeon uses cautery to achieve hemostasis near the vas deferens, carefully avoiding accidental division. The surgical field shows dissection across the midline, with the pubic symphysis visible on the right side of the body. The surgeon removes peritoneal structures and bladder fat from the vas, working to complete the critical view of the myopectomy orifice and ensuring the mesh can sit behind the pubis and cross the midline. The dissection extends into the retropubic space to expose the obturator fat plug.

The surgeon leaves the obturator fat plug in place, prioritizing coverage for a large indirect hernia. The internal ring is inspected for bleeding to avoid a scrotal hematoma. The corona mortis is visible, requiring further dissection to reach the obturator space. The mesh is positioned between the bladder, vas deferens, and external iliac vein, ensuring it sits vertically in the retropubic space for optimal posterior lateral coverage. A single 3-0 Vicryl suture is placed to Cooper's ligament, avoiding blindly suturing into fat.
